name: Coffee — extended (60 queries, stratified)
description: Decision-grade benchmark suite. 36 stratified core queries (3 sizes × 4 intents × 3 each), 12 adversarial cases (off-topic, vague, contradictory, no-RAG), and 12 deep-specific cases (recipes, troubleshooting, named products, FAQs). Each query carries optional `gold` fields used by deterministic assertions.

I just inherited my grandfather's old Italian espresso setup but I have no idea what I'm doing. He had a moka pot, a manual lever machine that looks ancient, and bags of beans I don't recognize. Where should I even start to learn?

I'm a tea drinker who's getting into coffee because everyone in my new office is a coffee snob. I don't want to spend a fortune but I want to actually understand what I'm tasting. What should I try first to develop my palate?

We just moved into a new place and want to set up a small coffee bar. Our budget is around $2000 total and we drink mostly espresso drinks but occasionally pour-over on weekends. What machine, grinder, and accessories should we look at?

Looking for a coffee setup for our small office of about 10 people. We need something easy for everyone to use, makes consistent espresso drinks, and doesn't require a barista. Budget is flexible if it saves time.

I'm choosing between buying a high-end manual espresso machine plus separate grinder, or a fully automatic super-auto. I make 3-4 drinks a day, mostly cappuccinos, and I'd rather spend 5 minutes total than 5 minutes per drink. Which path makes sense?

We're deciding between a Breville Dual Boiler, a Lelit Mara X, and a Profitec Pro 500 in the $1500-2500 range. We mostly do milk drinks, occasional black espresso, and want it to last 10+ years. Which would you put your money on and why?

I've been pulling shots on my Breville for two years and I'm wondering if I'm missing something with single-origin beans. Can you walk me through how the same recipe tastes different across origins, and recommend a few starter beans to try?

My partner only drinks decaf but I want them to enjoy real espresso. Most decaf I've had tastes flat — are there decaf options that hold up in a double shot, and does the brewing technique need to change at all?

I want to gift a complete pour-over kit to my brother who's just getting into specialty coffee. He has nothing but a kettle. Looking at $300 budget — what brewer, grinder, scale, and a starter bean would you put together?

I make pour-over every morning but my coffee always tastes either too bitter or too sour. I'm using a V60 with medium-fine grind and 1:16 ratio. What variables should I be adjusting and in what order?
My espresso looks beautiful — perfect crema, golden stream, runs in 28 seconds — but tastes weak and watery. I'm at 18g in, 36g out. What's actually happening and how do I diagnose?

I want to start tracking my espresso shots more rigorously. What variables actually matter, what should I write down, and how do I avoid getting lost in numbers and forgetting that the shot should taste good?
